mechanism_narrative: An extremely low federal funds rate, held near the zero lower bound, reduces the discount rate for corporate earnings and forces investors into riskier assets like equities. This provides a persistent tailwind for the S&P 500.
evidence_refs: fact:Federal Reserve initiated Zero Interest Rate Policy (ZIRP) in December 2008.
failure_condition: The average 60-day forward return for the S&P 500 is not statistically higher when FEDFUNDS is below 0.25% compared to periods when it is above 2%.

mechanism_narrative: A deceleration in the rate of increase of the unemployment rate signals that the worst of the recession's impact on the labor market has passed. This acts as a leading indicator for economic recovery, causing investors to bid up equity prices in anticipation.
candidate_trigger: 3-month change in UNRATE is positive but less than the prior 3-month change.
evidence_refs: fact:Unemployment rate rose from 4.7% to 8.9% in the preceding 18 months.
failure_condition: In triggered instances, the S&P 500 fails to produce positive 20-day forward returns in more than 50% of cases over a 3-year walk-forward test.

mechanism_narrative: The combination of aggressive monetary easing (low FEDFUNDS) and a bottoming in the housing market (stabilizing HOUST) creates a powerful positive impulse for equities. Low rates make mortgages affordable while a stable housing market restores household wealth and confidence, driving a broad recovery.
candidate_trigger: FEDFUNDS < 1% AND 3-month average of HOUST is greater than its 12-month average.
evidence_refs: fact:The 2008 crisis was centered on a housing market collapse.
failure_condition: A regression model predicting 60-day returns shows an insignificant (p > 0.10) coefficient for the interaction term between the FEDFUNDS level and the HOUST trend signal.

mechanism_narrative: A sustained increase in industrial production indicates that the supply side of the economy is healing and corporate activity is expanding. This directly translates into higher expected revenues and earnings, supporting higher equity valuations.
evidence_refs: fact:Industrial production experienced one of its sharpest declines on record in 2008-2009.
failure_condition: The 3-month change in INDPRO does not exhibit a rank information coefficient greater than 0.03 with 20-day forward S&P 500 returns over any rolling 3-year period.

mechanism_narrative: A sharp, unexpected reversal in payrolls, where job losses accelerate again after a period of moderation, signals a 'double-dip' recession. This triggers a rapid reassessment of recovery prospects and causes a sharp, negative market reaction.
candidate_trigger: Monthly change in PAYEMS is negative and below -250k after a prior month with a change greater than -250k.
evidence_refs: fact:Monthly job losses exceeded 700k in early 2009, creating high sensitivity to labor data.
failure_condition: When the trigger condition is met, the S&P 500 5-day forward returns are positive in more than 50% of instances.

mechanism_narrative: An acceleration in retail sales growth provides direct evidence of strengthening consumer demand, which accounts for a majority of the US economy. This leads to upward revisions in corporate earnings forecasts, particularly for consumer-facing sectors, driving the broader market higher.
evidence_refs: fact:Consumer spending collapsed during the 2008 crisis.
failure_condition: The 3-month percentage change in RSAFS does not have a statistically significant positive correlation with 20-day forward S&P 500 returns.

mechanism_narrative: In a deeply depressed economy, a turn from deflation or disinflation towards positive inflation is bullish for equities. It signals that aggregate demand is recovering, eases the burden of corporate debt, and confirms that monetary policy is gaining traction.
candidate_trigger: Year-over-year CPIAUCSL change rises for two consecutive months from a level below 1%.
evidence_refs: fact:Widespread market fears of a deflationary spiral exist in early 2009.
failure_condition: Following a trigger, the S&P 500's average 60-day forward return is not higher than its unconditional average return over the full sample.

mechanism_narrative: The interaction between a high unemployment rate and falling inflation creates a negative feedback loop. High unemployment suppresses wage growth and demand, which in turn pushes inflation lower (or negative), increasing real debt burdens and further chilling economic activity, leading to negative equity returns.
failure_condition: During periods when the trigger condition is active, the S&P 500 posts positive 60-day returns more than 55% of the time.
